description:
You are the on-call incident responder.  
   A suspicious host with the IPv4 address 203.0.113.5 has been scanning the perimeter and SOC wants a **single, ready-to-run iptables command** captured in a tiny helper script so it can be reviewed and then executed by the networking team.

   Your assignment:

   1. Make sure the directory /home/user/firewall exists (create it if it does not).
   2. Inside that directory create a new file named block_suspicious_ip.sh
   3. The file must contain exactly two non-empty lines, in this exact order and with no extra leading/trailing spaces or blank lines:
      • Line 1: #!/bin/sh  
      • Line 2: iptables -A INPUT -s 203.0.113.5 -j DROP
   4. Give the script executable permissions for the owner (mode 700 is acceptable, 755 is also acceptable, but it **must** be executable by the owner).
   5. Do not place anything else in that directory and do not modify any other files.
   6. When you are done, list the long format (ls -l) of /home/user/firewall so the security lead can quickly verify the script exists and is executable.

   Automated test checklist (the grader will check all of the following):
   • A regular file named /home/user/firewall/block_suspicious_ip.sh exists.  
   • The file has owner-executable permission (the x bit for the owner must be set).  
   • The file contains **exactly** the two lines shown above, with a single newline character terminating each line and no additional blank lines.  
   • The directory /home/user/firewall contains no other files or sub-directories.

   Produce only the necessary terminal interaction to fulfil these requirements.
truth:
Path that must exist after completion:
     /home/user/firewall/block_suspicious_ip.sh

   Required final permissions (symbolic representation accepted by ls -l):
     -rwx------

   Exact file content (with Unix LF endings):
     #!/bin/sh\n
     iptables -A INPUT -s 203.0.113.5 -j DROP\n
name: task_000000_fc37c10e
